narrative: As a white male applicant with a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.10 (well below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 23.50 (far under the usual 36.70), and Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the bottom 10th percentile (compared to mid-range averages around 5.5)—and predicted a 97% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high failure risk directly led to the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar without delays. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 academic decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 academic decile, all of which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure rather than success. Your Year 3 decile ranking in the lowest tenth of your school had the single biggest negative impact, signaling to the model a significant drop-off or persistent weakness in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Closely behind, your low LSAT score reinforced concerns about your readiness for the rigors of law school and the bar, as it's a key predictor far below what succeeds for most students. Even your Year 1 decile at the bottom amplified these worries, showing early academic challenges that didn't improve. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with mid-to-high deciles, stronger GPAs, and LSATs in the 30s or above, your metrics placed you in a high-risk category where bar passage rates are historically low. Your full-time enrollment plan and family income didn't sway the outcome much here, as they weren't major drivers. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or a master's program, and targeting schools with bridge programs for lower-decile applicants. Building a narrative of academic turnaround in reapplications, perhaps with letters highlighting your resilience as a first-generation or lower-middle income student, could help too—many have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps head-on. Keep pushing; law school doors reopen for determined applicants who improve their profiles.
